coredata.dc:title: Efficient generation of hydrogen from NaBH4 catalyzed by nickel-decorated magnetite-carbon dots/nanodiamonds template

coredata.dc:description: This paper describes synthesis and characterization of CDs/NDs and its CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nanocomposite. The nanomaterials are characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), elemental mapping analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), N2 absorption-desorption (BET) analysis, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), and gas chromatography (GC) analysis. Here, a high catalytic activity is discovered for the synthesized CDs/NDs towards NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction. The magnetically separable CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni is synthesized which overcome the instability of the CDs/NDs and shows a perfect catalytic activity in proximity with the highest values reported in the literature with hydrogen generation rate of 80000 mLH2·min−1·gcat −1 and furnish high efficiencies. At higher temperatures, the accompanying water-splitting side reaction led by the CDs/NDs system causes over-expected volumes of H2 production.

head.ce:title: Efficient generation of hydrogen from NaBH4 catalyzed by nickel-decorated magnetite-carbon dots/nanodiamonds template

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, 5756151818, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, 5756151818, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes synthesis and characterization of CDs/NDs and its CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nanocomposite. The nanomaterials are characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), elemental mapping analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), N2 absorption-desorption (BET) analysis, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), and gas chromatography (GC) analysis. Here, a high catalytic activity is discovered for the synthesized CDs/NDs towards NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction. The magnetically separable CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni is synthesized which overcome the instability of the CDs/NDs and shows a perfect catalytic activity in proximity with the highest values reported in the literature with hydrogen generation rate of 80000 mLH2·min−1·gcat −1 and furnish high efficiencies. At higher temperatures, the accompanying water-splitting side reaction led by the CDs/NDs system causes over-expected volumes of H2 production.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuel reduction and environmental pollution are the fundamental concerns of energy crises more than ever. Therefore, renewable energy sources should be developed. Because of its high energy density (∼140 MJ/kg), sustainability, socioeconomic advantages and cleanliness, hydrogen is noted as an excellent successor of fossil fuels among the modern energy technologies [1–4]. It is anticipated to be broadly used in electricity generation, heating, mechanical power and transportation [5]. Maturing of hydrogen as fuel confronted with many challenges. Hydrogen production and storage are two major challenges that researchers are working on. There are many procedures to obtain hydrogen, such as water electrolysis [6], natural gas reforming [7], coal gasification [8], ethanol steam reforming [9] and hydrolysis of hydrazine borane [10] and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) [11]. However, there is much to obtain an optimal procedure [12].

Depending on the essence of the interaction between the stored hydrogen and the storage container or material, current hydrogen storage technologies are classified into three main branches [13,14]: 1) molecular hydrogen can be kept as gas or a liquid without remarkable physical or chemical bonding to other materials; for example, H2 is stored in pressurized vessels and liquefied hydrogen tanks [15], 2) molecular hydrogen may be physically adsorbed onto or into a material and held by relatively weak van der Waals bonds. For example, different porous adsorbents are applied in this category [16], 3) hydrogen may be adsorbed chemically and stored in the form of atomic hydrogen. Interstitial hydrides (MH) such as alanetes (MAlH4) and borohydrides (MBH4) and chemical hydrides, including metalloboron hydrides, ammonia borane, formic acid, hydrazine hydrate, and aromatic compounds are the subdivisions of this category that are distinguished based on the chemical bonding. Hydrogen storage in materials offers sustainable hydrogen storage systems and enables hydrogen "supply on demand". They can supply the requirements for industrial applications [17].

Owing to the outstanding advantages such as high hydrogen storage capacity, controllability of hydrolysis, stability and high hydrogen purity, borohydrides are considered very promising hydrogen storage materials [18–22]. Sodium borohydride [23,24] is an encouraging candidate of borohydrides, which generates hydrogen via pyrolysis, hydrolysis and methanolysis [12,25,26]. NaBH4 has attracted researchers’ attention due to various reasons: it has high gravimetric hydrogen storage capacity (GHSC) about 10.8 wt % and is cheap compared to other hydrogen storage materials [27]. It involves significant progress of reaction kinetics under ambient condition. The solution of NaBH4 is nonflammable and by-product of NaBH4 decomposition is NaBO2 which is recyclable and non-toxic. Pure H2 is produced and the hydrogen extraction rate can be controlled by different reaction parameters [2,28,29].

Hydrolysis reaction of sodium borohydride is applicable in various fields, including chemistry, biomedical, materials science, engineering [30], environmental sections [31–33], and fuel cells [34]. Stoichiometrically, 90% of hydrogen can be released from a solution of NaBH4 through the hydrolysis reaction. In addition, NaBH4 can be regarded as a water-splitting agent, because 50% of the generated hydrogen is produced from water [4]. The reaction of NaBH4 with H2O is given as:

However, the self-hydrolysis of NaBH4 without a catalyst is extremely slow. Self-hydrolysis reaction at ambient condition is eventually discontinued and precedes only 7–8%. Therefore, the design and synthesis of effective catalysts for releasing hydrogen with acceptable rates is highly required [35–37].

Effective catalysts provide reactants with catalytic active sites which are essential to promote the reactions efficiently. In this regard, nanoparticles achieved great interest because they provide high surface areas, abundant quantity of active sites and unique electronic properties. Nano-catalysts bring together the advantages of homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts and overcome their disadvantages. However, the nanosized catalysts tend to aggregate due to their higher surface free energy and so their catalytic performance decline. In addition, separation and recovery of nano-catalysts is hard and, in most cases, the usual filtration appears to be impossible. The most reasonable solution to these problems involves the application of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs). A magnetized nano-catalyst can easily and effectually be isolated from the reaction system by using a magnet, and can be reutilized in other cycles. Moreover, suitable surface-modifications can be carried out on MNPs to fabricate stable and highly dispersed active particles. Cobalt, nickel, and iron are all ferromagnetic materials, and it has been suggested that this property could be used as a catalytic “on/off” switch by using a magnet to remove the catalyst from the solution when hydrogen generation is no longer required. Magnetic nano-catalysts of nano metal oxides (NMOs) such as iron oxides (FeO, Fe2O3, and Fe3O4), spinel ferrites (MFe2O4), and modified magnetic nanoparticles are used widely [38,39].

Currently, catalysts with high efficiencies for hydrogen production via hydrolysis usually contain supported noble metals, such as Pt/C, Pt–Ru/metal oxide, Pd/C, Ru/anion-exchange resin, Ag–Ni core-shell nanoparticles (NPs), Au–Co bi-metals, and Co–Pt core-shell nanoparticles and rhodium [40]. However, the use of these catalysts is restricted due to the high cost and limited resource for noble metals. The low-cost catalysts such as cobalt [41] were also broadly used in the hydrolysis of NaBH4 however their catalytic activity is far less than that of noble metal catalysts. Overall, there exists still a significant distance to obtain catalysts with large-scale practical application. Therefore, it is essential to construct efficient low-cost catalysts for large-scale industrial production of hydrogen from sodium borohydride hydrolysis.

Carbon dots (CDs) and nanodiamonds (NDs) that are considered outstanding members of carbon nanomaterials family, has been under increasing emphasized studies on their synthesis, characteristics and applications.

Recently, many research studies have been introduced on properties and potential applications of carbon dots. CDs are classified into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). They have appeared as significant members of novel nanomaterials, and own outstanding characteristics such as negligible cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, chemical and physical responsiveness, remarkable chemical inertness, resistance to photo- and chemical-bleaching, abundant functional groups (e.g., amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl), and electron mobility. In addition, they have unique fluorescent properties, and are low cost. Therefore, CDs acquired a wide range of applications in photocatalysis, production of polychromatic LEDs, biological and medicinal fields, energy conversion and storage branches, etc. [42–44].

In addition to carbon dots, nanodiamonds (NDs) also benefit from exceptional properties such as low toxicity, eco-friendliness, strong resistance under harsh conditions, surface-rich functional groups, impressive electrochemical properties, high thermal conductivity, excellent light absorption, extreme hardness and mechanical resistance, structural tunability at the atomic/morphological level, which make it suitable for application in various conditions. NDs have been extensively studied in different areas such as imaging, catalysis, energy conversion, sensing, quantum computing, electrochemistry, drug delivery, lubricants, wastewater treatment, etc. NDs can be subdivided on the basis of their primary particles’ size for example into nanocrystalline diamonds (NCDs) (10–150 nm), ultra-nanocrystalline diamonds (UNCDs) (2–10 nm) and diamondoids (1–2 nm) [45–47].

Surveying the literature shows carbon dot catalysts have been applied in some studies to expedite the NabH4 hydrolysis reactions. For example, Carbon dot-Co3O4 [48,49], CQD-Ni/NiO [50], GQDs-TMNPs [51], B/N co-doped CDs [52] are among the catalysts whose efficiencies were reported [53]. However, no nanodiamond based material has been reported as catalyst of NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction in literature. But, Rhodium/nanodiamond hybrid was utilized to catalyze the hydrogen production from ammonia–borane where nanodiamond was showed to be an ideal matrix [54].

In line with the outlined strategies and in continue of our previous works [55–57], here we synthesize carbon dots/nanodiamonds (CDs/NDs) composition using a modified hummers’ method that is generally used for GO synthesis. The synthesized CDs/NDs composition showed high activity in catalyzing NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction. Such catalytical activity was not reported for carbon materials before. However, this CDs/NDs catalyst is unstable, so CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni is synthesized as a relatively stable and highly active nano-catalyst of the hydrolysis reaction wherein CDs/NDs scaffolds synergistically cooperated with Fe3O4 and Ni to catalyze hydrolysis of NaBH4. This report demonstrates that CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni generates hydrogen gas from sodium borohydride efficiently and expeditiously, and is a promising conformation to accelerate the hydrolysis reaction of NaBH4. In addition, in relevance to our previous work [55], it is shown that the CDs/NDs composition retained its water-splitting power in the here synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni composite somewhat, and due to this property, that leads water splitting side reactions, over-expected H2 volumes are generated in some experiments.

All chemicals were purchased from commercial sources and were utilized without any purification. A Thermo Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer recorded the Fourier-transform infrared spectra of the materials. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were performed on a ZEISS SIGMA VP FESEM apparatus. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were carried out using FEI Tecnai G2 F20 SuperTwin TEM at accelerating voltage of 200 kV and Philips BioTwin, the Netherlands, at 75 kV, respectively. To obtain TEM images, samples were mounted on formvar coated copper grids as per the standard procedure. A X'Pert Pro Panalytical diffractometer was used for Xray diffraction (XRD) measurements in 40 kV/30 mA with a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å), and the signal data were recorded in 2θ = 10°–80° with a step interval of 0.05°. Linseis STA PT-1000, Germany, instrument recorded the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) pattern of the samples. Nova Station B, Quantachrome Instrument, USA measured the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, pore volume, and pore diameter of the specimens. A Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) made by Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir Co. Iran was applied to specify the magnetic features of the samples under magnetic field up to 20 kOe. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) on Optima 7300D determined the wt % of metals on the synthesized nano-catalyst. PL (photoluminescence) pattern was obtained with 365 nm excitation at r. t. Using a PerkinElmer FL 6500, USA aperture. Gas chromatography (GC) analysis was performed using a molecular sieve 5 Å packed column (Teif Gostar Faraz, Iran).

Graphene oxide was prepared using our modified Hummers’ method. In a 250 mL round-bottomed flask laid in an ice bath, graphite (0.5 g) and 25 mL sulfuric acid were mixed with a magnetic stirrer. While keeping the flask in the ice bath, NaNO3 (0.5 g) was added to the mixture and the stirring was continued for 4 h. Maintaining the ice-bath condition, the reaction system was gradually blended with KMnO4 (3 g) and the system was stirred for 20 min. After undergoing a 50 min of sonication process, the mixture was stirred for 2 h at 40 °C. Next, 46 mL deionized water was added to the blend in a 500 mL flask. The process was continued by stirring the mixture for 2 h at 90–95 °C. Then, the heater was turned off and 100 mL deionized water was poured into the reaction flask. Finally, a constant stirring for 1 h was exerted to the system after adding 10 mL H2O2 (30%). The expected brown graphene oxide was isolated by centrifugation of the final yellow mixture. The product underwent a washing process by HCl (5%), H2O2 and then was dried for 48 h in 50 °C.

To a blend of FeCl3·6H2O (4 mmol, 1.081 g) and FeCl2·4H2O (2 mmol, 0.4 g) in a 500 mL round-bottomed flask, 100 mL H2O was added. After dissolving the chemicals, a dispersion of CDs/NDs in water (50 mL, 0.55 mg/mL) was added. The mixture was sonicated until a homogenous blend was obtained. Next, the reaction system was put under N2 atmosphere. Subsequently, the heater was set at 80 °C and aqueous ammonia (25%) was gradually added until the reaction mixture reached a pH ∼ 10 whiles being stirred. The system was stirred and refluxed for 4 h. Finally, the black precipitated product was separated using an external magnetic field and was washed and dried at 60 °C for 48 h [58].

Ni(OAc)2·4H2O (1 mmol, 0.249 g) was dispersed in 30 mL deionized water by sonication in a 500 mL round-bottomed flask. A dispersion of the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 (0.708 g) in 100 mL of deionized water was added into the 500 mL flask under stirring at 80 °C. Next, 25 mL N2H4·H2O was added to the reaction system that was set under a stream of N2. In order to reach pH 13, NaOH solution (1 M) was gradually added to the reaction mixture. Ultimately, refluxing is carried out for 4 h. Afterwards, the nano-catalyst was collected using a magnet and purified by washing with water and EtOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A general procedure for generation of H2 from NaBH4 catalyzed by CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the volume of the released gas, a graduated burette equipped with a gas inlet was applied. The graduated burette was bought and a gas inlet was installed to the instrument. According to Fig. 3, using a hose, the gas inlet of the burette was connected to a 25 ml round-bottomed flask located on a heater-stirrer. The aperture of the burette was immersed into a soap solution so that soapsuds could be drawn into the burette as volume indicators. A hose was also connected to the upper hole of the burette to help the injection of the soapsuds into the graduated burette by suction and control the suds level. This upper hole of the burette acted as excess gas outlet, too.

The synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni and NaBH4 were mixed in a 25 mL round-bottomed flask. Then, the flask was located on a heater-stirrer that was set on a particular test temperature. Afterwards, 10 mL of hot deionized water with an equal temperature to the heater was added. Subsequently, the volume of the released gas and the time required to complete the hydrolysis reaction were determined according to the data extracted from the videos recorded from the experiments. To measure the produced volume of hydrogen, the unites of volume that were traversed by soapsuds were registered for every ascertained period of minutes. For any seconds of time that there were no soapsuds in the instrument to reveal the hydrogen volume and hydrogen production rate (blind periods), the rate was registered based on the immediate previous and pursuing seconds as the following: a) when the hydrogen generation rate was equal in the previous and the pursuing seconds of the blind period, the hydrogen generation rate and hydrogen volume was registered equal to those amounts. b) when the hydrogen generation rate was lower in the pursuing second(s) of the blind period, the hydrogen generation rate and hydrogen volume was registered equal to the pursuing lower amount.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyzing FT-IR pattern of the synthesized CDs/NDs composition
ce:section.ce:para: According to the FT-IR spectrum (Appx. Fig. 1 ), the synthesized CDs/NDs composition frameworks contain plenty of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxide groups. The peaks at 3397 cm−1, 2926 cm−1 and 1725 cm−1 are assigned to the hydroxyl, CH2 and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The sharp peak at 1633 cm−1 can be associated to the vibrations of OH groups of the water adsorbed onto the CDs/NDs and can also be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC. The peaks appeared at 1375 cm−1, 1259 cm−1 and 1104 cm−1 are corresponded to C–O–H deformation vibrations, C–O–C and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM, EDX, HR-TEM, and PL analyses of CDs/NDs composition
ce:section.ce:para:
In accordance with the low magnification SEM image, the obtained CDs/NDs composition consists of stacked fractured slabs. Based on the high-magnification SEM image, lamellar, cracked and wrinkled nanosheets formed the CDs/NDs composition scaffolds (Appx. Fig. 2
). The nano-fractured fragments were gathered together in assemblies in the range of a few micrometers. These assemblies can be related to the oxygenated groups. The EDX analysis (Appx. Fig. 3) showed that the CDs/NDs composition was constructed from approximately 72.5 wt% C and 27.5 wt% O.

To characterize the morphology and structure of the synthesized graphene oxide, high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) experiments were done (Fig. 1). HR-TEM images and SAEDs verify that the obtained CDs/NDs composition is in forms of single– or few–layer nano-crystals. The TEM images reveal high crystallinity feature of the CDs and NDs composition. As showed in the TEM images in Fig. 1a and b, the obtained CDs/NDs composition crystals have got different shapes. The crystallites were shaped as parallelogram-type rectangles with rounded vertices and deformed sides, and circular and elliptical CDs/NDs with deformed curved sides. Minor irregular shaped particles (amorphous carbon structures) are also displayed in this image [59].

According to the high resolved (HR-TEM) images shown in Fig. 1d–f, the honey comb structure of graphite sheets was removed in the synthesized CDs/NDs composition, and the lattice unites of the crystallites were formed by parallel strings (carbon atomic chains) of different heights, rather than the expected hexagonal unit cells. Lattices made of parallel carbon atomic chains are mostly observed in lattice structures of nanodiamonds [60–62]. According to the depicted magnified images from Fig. 1d (Fig. 1e and f), random covalent bonds between atoms of the chains are observable across the lattices [60,63]. Covalent bonds are also observed at the endings of the lattices where rectangular lattice unites were made as a result. These depicted images from Fig. 1d also illustrate that the scaffolds of the crystallites are composed of single-layer and few-layer lattices. According to Fig. 1f, these layers were connected by covalent bonds. In Fig. 1f, broken bonds of atoms in the length of the chains of a lattice are arrayed which formed new bonds with the atoms from the met lattice, at those locations. This bond formations were occurred along the broken length of the final chains of a lattice with terminal atoms of a side neighbor lattice which had chains with cross direction. As well, bond formations are predictable between lattices that are located above and below of an assumed reference lattice. This phenomenon can be confirmed by the broken chains of the lattices above which other layers are observed. The space between two chains (d-spacing) in Fig. 1e was measured to be 0.168 nm that is reported for nanodiamonds [64] D-spacings of 0.324 nm and 0.304 nm were obtained for the insider and terminal lattice unites, respectively, in Fig. 1f. A recolored image extracted from Fig. 1f is shown in Scheme 1 which illustrates the carbon atomic chains of the grids and their connections.

Fig. 1c shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the representative image in Fig. 1b. The SAED pattern reveals distinct diffraction spots arranged in ring like pattern. This pattern shows a polycrystalline structure [65,66]. The broadened diffraction spots indicate that some nanocrystals in the particle are aligned along similar crystallographic direction [67,68].

The interplanar spacings (d) estimated from the SAED pattern using Image J FIJI software showed the d-spacings of 0.356, 0.240, 0.193, 0.171, 0.150, 0.138, 0.128, and 0.119 nm from inner ring to the outmost ring, respectively. The d-spacing 0.356 nm (hkl = 002) that is slightly larger than d-spacing of bulk graphite (0.336 nm), can be attributed to the graphene oxide quantum dots in the sample [69]. Also, it can represent the unit cubic cell in a nanodiamond lattice (hkl = 100) [70]. D-spacing 0.193 nm and 0.150 nm can be correlated to hexagonal nanodiamonds (101) (JCPDS19-0268) and (102) (JCPDS19-0268) planes, respectively. The interplanar spacing of 0.128 nm and 0.171 can be corresponded to n-nanodiamonds (220) and (200) planes, orderly [71]. The interplanar spacing of 0.128 nm can also be correlated to i-carbon (200) planes [72]. D-spacing of 0.240 nm can be corresponded to i-carbons (111) planes [73]. D-spacings of 0.138 and 0.119 nm are apparently new phases of carbon.

Based on these explanations and the XRD diffraction pattern analysis (Appx. Fig. 4 ), the synthesized material was a mixture of nanodiamonds and carbon dots such as graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), i-carbon, and/or connections of those scaffolds.

As mentioned in literature, carbon dots (CDs) and nanodiamonds (NDs) have photoluminescence. To ascertain the existence of this characteristic in the synthesized CDs/NDs composition, PL analysis was performed at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the corresponding sharp peak was observed at 413.5 nm (Fig. 1g).

In the XRD pattern (Appx. Fig. 4) of the synthesized CDs/NDs, the characteristic strong and sharp diffraction peak of graphite at around 2θ = 26.55° [74–76] (JCPDS 41–1487) was removed and a wide peak at 2θ = 11.18° was appeared instead. This difference indicates the insertion of oxygen functional groups into the graphite layers. The characteristic low and broad peak of rGO between 2θ = 20 and 40° (JCPDS 03–0401) is also observed in the XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs/NDs which can be correlated to the GOQDs present in the composition. Based on this observation, it is deducible that a portion of other components in the synthesized CDs/NDs composition must have also been reduced during the reaction process [77,78]. In addition, this peak at 2θ = 20–40° is also attributed to carbon dots [79].

The peak at 2θ = 42.63° can be correlated to nano-diamond structures (JCPDS card 79–1467) [80–82]. Therefore, this XRD pattern confirms that the synthesized catalyst was a mixture of nanodiamonds and carbon dots such as graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), i-carbon, and/or connections of those scaffolds.

To investigate the thermal stability of the synthesized CDs/NDs, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Appx. Fig. 5 ) was utilized. The analysis was fulfilled under O2 flaw in the 30–70 °C range. This analysis indicated the synthesized CDs/NDs decompose mainly in three stages in the temperature ranges of 30–146.8 °C, 146.8–224.0 °C, 224.0–700.4 °C. The pattern shows that CDs/NDs had lost around 11.36 % of its weight at the first stage, and then a sharp weight loss of 38.70 % had occurred. Besides removal of the absorbed water, these can be attributed to destruction of oxygen containing groups. The overall pattern implies a thermally unstable structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR analysis of Ni, Fe3O4, CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nanocomposite systems
ce:section.ce:para: According to FT-IR analysis of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nanocomposite systems (Appx. Fig. 6 ), after loading Fe3O4 and Ni nanoparticles within the CDs/NDs scaffold, most of the CDs/NDs bands were retained in the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni with different shifts. The peak of Fe–O vibrations corresponding to the Fe3O4 constituent of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni, was appeared at around 571 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of the nanocomposites. The broad peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 3426 cm−1 are assigned to the hydroxyl groups of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni systems, respectively. The OH vibrations of the adsorbed water as well as the CC vibrations that are related to the CDs/NDs substrate of the CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nano-systems, are observed at 1609 and 1574 cm−1, respectively. The CH2 stretching peaks of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 were appeared at 2922 cm−1 and those of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni are observed at 2921 cm−1. In the pattern of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni, the peak attributed to C–O–C fragments of CDs/NDs is manifest at 1213 cm−1. In the FT-IRs of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni, the carbonyl peak of CDs/NDs at 1725 cm−1 was vanished. Hence, being loaded by metallic constituents, CDs/NDs underwent reduction reactions.

According to the TEM image of the synthesized Fe3O4 nanoparticle were loaded on the CDs/NDs as the morphology and size distribution of CDs/NDs nanoparticles were clearly changed. Impregnation of CDs/NDs with Fe3O4 paved the way for distribution of different metals on the new catalyst support in the next step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM, EDX, ICP-OES, and TEM analyses of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni

ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni is depicted in Appx. Fig. 9 . The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.5°, 36°, 43.5°, 54.0°, 57.5° and 62.6° are correlated to the XRD pattern of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (JCPDS 19–0629). The broad peak observed in CDs/NDs XRD pattern at 2θ = 20–40° that is corresponded to GOQDs and CDs, has maintained in the XRD pattern of the nanocomposite and has overlapped with magnetite peaks. Additionally, the bands attributed to the Ni nanoparticles were depicted at 2θ = 45°, 47.5° and 74.5° (JCPDS 65–0380).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Appx. Fig. 12) was applied to analyze the thermal behavior of the CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni. This technique showed a significant weight loss that starts at 197.5 °C. A small weight loss is also observable (0.4 %) up to 100 °C which is assigned to the water evaporation. Therefore, the nano-catalyst is thermally stable up to 197.5 °C, and can satisfactorily work at temperatures below 100 °C that are considered in this work.

Using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) technique, magnetic feature of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni was analyzed in the applied external magnetic field up to 20 kOe (Appx. Fig. 13). According to the VSM curve, CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni sample became magnetized almost sharply in the presence of the applied external field and the magnetic changes were non-linear with a reversible character. Moreover, the magnetic property of the sample became zero when the magnetic field was removed. Therefore, based on the VSM analysis results CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni involved a superparamagnetic characteristic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overall assessing of the catalytic activity of CDs/NDs, CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni in the hydrolysis of NaBH4 to distinguish the most active one
ce:section.ce:para: To ascertain the activity of CDs/NDs, CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni in the hydrolysis of NaBH4, we conducted simple macroscopic examinations. First, the catalytic activity of CDs/NDs in hydrolysis of NaBH4 was tested. To carry out this experiment, 0.5 g of NaBH4 was completely mixed with the synthesized CDs/NDs in a mortar, and the mixture was put on a 60 °C heater-stirrer for 30 s. Then, deionized water was added which led to immediate and vigorous initiation of NaBH4 decomposition. Based on this test, the synthesized CDs/NDs had an intense catalytic activity in the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride. However, its catalytic activity was far less afterwards in the retest cycles that were conducted applying the washed and unwashed CDs/NDs compounds. This drastic diminishment in the catalytic activity of the synthesized CDs/NDs was mostly due to the simultaneous participation of its oxygenated groups in the reactions with the produced H2 during the hydrolysis reaction proceeding which practically changed the characteristics of CDs/NDs. The synthesized CDs/NDs had previously proved to be a good catalyst in carbonyl group reduction reactions in the presence of NaBH4 [56]. According to the thermal analysis of the synthesized CDs/NDs (Appx. Fig. 5), exposure to heat could also be another reason for depletion of CDs/NDs’ catalytic activity as heat destroyed the structure of CDs/NDs. Based on these, CDs/NDs was established as a highly active but unstable catalyst in the hydrolysis of NaBH4. In the literature, CDs/NDs have been reported as an efficient merely support or a support with a trivial catalytic activity for different catalysts of hydrolysis reactions and as an interested support for H2 storage materials; however, up to best of our knowledge CDs/NDs were not reported as catalysts of hydrolysis reactions by themselves. Our findings encourage studies for finding methods to stabilize CDs/NDs chemically and thermally along with maintaining its power in catalyzing NaBH4 hydrolysis and characterizing the features presented in this synthesized CDs/NDs that boosted the hydrolysis of NaBH4.

For this purpose, we fabricated CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 by loading Fe3O4 on the synthesized CDs/NDs as illustrated in 2.3 section. In addition to maintaining the catalytic activity of CDs/NDs in the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 nanocomposite, magnetic properties were also dwelled in this new system. Moreover, this nanocomposite inferably was thermally more stable than CDs/NDs because of hydrogen bonds formations throughout the nanocomposites' scaffold. To achieve an outstanding catalytic activity and to obtain more active sites, nickel nanoparticles were loaded on the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 template in an additional step, and as the ICP-OES analysis showed 4.64 wt% Ni nanoparticles were loaded on the previously synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 nano-catalyst. The overriding objectives of this fabrication, namely, conservation of CDs/NDs' catalytic activity and promoting the nano-catalysts’ stability, were well maintained in the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni architecture. As shown in Appx. Fig. 12, thermal stability of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni was precisely higher compared to the synthesized CDs/NDs. The thermal analysis showed the catalyst would be stable at temperatures below 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the catalytic activity of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the efficiency of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni in promoting the hydrolysis reaction of NaBH4, the following setup and different experimental conditions were applied. Videos of all experiments were recorded and the data were extracted from the videos.

A graduated burette equipped with a gas inlet was used to measure the volume of the gas released. According to Fig. 4, using a hose, the gas inlet of the burette was connected to a round-bottomed (25 mL) flask located on a heater-stirrer. The aperture of the burette was immersed into a soap solution so that soapsuds could be drawn into the burette as volume indicators. A hose was also connected to the upper hole of the burette to help the injection of the soapsuds into the graduated burette by suction and control the suds level. This upper hole of the burette acted as excess released gas outlet, too.

Different amounts of sodium borohydride (0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 g) and the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni (0.008 and 0.016 g) were mixed in a round-bottomed (25 mL) flask. Then, the flask was located on a heater-stirrer that was set on a particular test temperature (r.t., 50, or 80 °C). Afterwards, 10 mL of the deionized water with an equal temperature to the presumed initiation reaction temperature (r.t., 50 and 80 °C) was poured into the flask and immediately the flask was connected to the graduated burette of the previously prepared setup. Finally, the volume of the released gas and the time required to complete the hydrolysis reaction were determined according to the data extracted from the recorded videos of the experiments. The results were summarized as the graphs presented in Figs. 5–9.

According to the obtained graphs (Fig. 5−9), the rate of hydrogen production from NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction in the presence of the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni ascended to a maximum level first and then the rate was gradually descended. For example, the average rate of H2 production from 0.3 g NaBH4 and 10 mL deionized water in the presence of 0.008 g CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni at 50 °C (Fig. 6) in different intervals is presented in Table 1 and the rate ascending-descending trend is clearly observable here, too. According to this table, this hydrolysis reaction completed in 18 min, and the highest rate of H2 production was within the 8−10th minutes (210 mL/2 min
≅
105 mL/min) where 210 mL H2 was obtained. As observed in the table, the higher amount of the total produced H2 gas (649 mL) was obtained up to the 10th minute and then the produced amounts descended gradually. On the basis of the details brought below, the reasons for these behaviors can be summarized as the following: Hydrogen production rate increase can be due to the temperature increase resulted from the hydrolysis reaction promotion which progressively released higher amount of energy. Moreover, higher concentration of NaBH4 was available for the nano-catalyst moieties at the preliminary steps, and therefore more reaction proceedings were reached at those steps. These proceedings led to higher temperatures and as a result more hydrogen volume was obtained from the decomposition processes at the early minutes. On the contrary, the continuous depletion of H2 production rate after a maximum point in these graphs can be correlated to the reduction of NaBH4 amount during these processes and also to the continuous decrease of the temperatures that were occurred because of continuous lowering of the released energies from the decreasing amounts of NaBH4.

The rate increasing trends are precisely observed in Fig. 5−8. However, such an early-stage gradual hydrogen volume increase is not observed in Fig. 9. This can be correlated to the lower amount of NaBH4 applied in this experiment and also to the measuring system set that was loosely sealed. Lower amount of NaBH4 produced lower amount of H2 and the loosely sealed measuring system allowed the produced hydrogen gas leak out of the system. It can be said that for 0.1 g NaBH4 decomposition reaction in 10 mL water in the presence of 0.008 nano-catalyst at 80 °C, the volume of H2 from the initial reaction steps didn't have enough pressure and couldn't be measured by the measurement system. The high volume of the obtained hydrogen in Figs. 5–8 is a demonstrator for existing initial missed part in Fig. 9 and shows that the produced gas volume was maybe much more in this experiment. Based on these explanations, gas leak from the measurement system is also presumable for the other experimental measurements carried out in this work and therefore the gas volumes evolved from all of the presented experiments here maybe were much higher.

Another point shown on these experiments is the higher than the theoretically expected volume of hydrogen gas (343 mL) produced from 0.1 g NaBH4 decomposition reaction in deionized water in the presence of 0.016 g of the nano-catalyst at 80 °C (Fig. 8) that is distinguishable from the reaction that was carried out using 0.1 g NaBH4, 0.008 g of the nano-catalyst and 10 mL deionized water at 80 °C (Fig. 9). In the former, the amount of the applied nano-catalyst was 2 times more and the obtained gas volume was enormously higher compared to the H2 volume (62 mL) in the later experiment. In addition, the highest hydrogen production rate in the experiment with 0.016 g of the nano-catalyst achieved 6313 mL min−1·gcat −1 while the highest rate with the 0.008 g of the catalyst reached 3125 mL min−1·gcat −1. These comparisons demonstrate the cruciality and importance of the amount of the nano-catalyst in the hydrolysis reaction rate and concludes that higher amount of the applied nano-catalyst results higher rate of NaBH4 hydrolysis. This importance can be explained by the fact that with increasing the amount of the nano-catalyst, more sodium borohydride molecules are contacted with the nano-catalyst and involved in the hydrolysis reactions. However, this conclusion cannot be generalized for all concentrations of the nano-catalyst. Comparing these two experiments also denotes the gas leak from the system.

According to data, temperature was an effective factor on the hydrolysis reactions as well, and the hydrogen production rate increased with temperature. Explicitly, the hydrolysis reaction of 0.3 g NaBH4 in the presence of 0.008 g of the nano-catalyst and 10 mL deionized water at 50 °C (Fig. 6) was fulfilled within 18 min while with the same amount of the materials, the decomposition reaction completion at room temperature (r.t., Fig. 5) lasted for 71 min. However, the high and approximate final amount of the gas obtained from these two experimental reactions were close which concludes the efficiency of the synthesized nano-catalyst in decomposing sodium borohydride in water. Moreover, the highest hydrogen production rates at 50 °C and room temperature were 13125 and 2475 mL min−1·gcat −1 (respectively) which further confirm the dependance of hydrolysis rate on temperature.

The effect of concentration of NabH4 in the reaction media on the rate of the decomposition reaction can also be considered by some estimates. By taking into account the above-mentioned positive effect of the catalyst's amount and based on Table 2 , the experimental data of this work show that the hydrolysis rate was increased with increasing the concentration of NaBH4. This conclusion can be got by comparing the experimental data for the hydrolysis of 0.5 g NaBH4 in the presence of 0.008 g of the nano-catalyst and 10 mL deionized water at 80 °C (Fig. 7) which reached the maximal hydrolysis rate of 80000 mL min−1·gcat −1and the data for the hydrolysis of 0.1 g NaBH4 in the presence of 0.016 g of the nano-catalyst and 10 mL deionized water at 80 °C (Fig. 8) where the applied nano-catalyst amount was twice and the highest reaction rate reached 6313 mL min−1·gcat −1. Clearly, it is concluded that higher concentrations of sodium borohydride were decomposed with higher rates.

The next matter as found from Figs. 7 and 8, and the related data in Table 2 is the volumes of the produced gas from the corresponding decomposition reactions that are higher than the volumes expected according to equation (1). The difference can be related to water splitting reactions that occurred in the presence of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nano-catalyst. The water-splitting character of this nano-catalyst majorly originates from the CDs/NDs constituent. To confirm the water-splitting power of CDs/NDs moieties refer to our previous work [55].

Reduction of H2 production rate after a maximum point in these graphs can be attributed to the reduction of NaBH4 amount which was accompanied with the reduction of the amount of the energy released from the hydrolysis reaction. As mentioned above, the released energy raises the temperature of the reaction system and the higher temperature raises the hydrolysis rate more. After a peak point of H2 production rate where the temperature is the highest, the energy release from the hydrolysis decreases with NaBH4 decrease and leads to the decrease of the reaction system temperature and lowering of the hydrolysis rate as a result.

By comparing the hydrolysis data obtained from the reactions led by the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni with data from the references brought in Table 2, the CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nano-catalyst can be proposed as an efficient nano-catalyst for hydrolysis of NaBH4 in terms of hydrogen yield and the reaction rate. For example, the most effective catalyst reported so far involves Ru(0) nanoparticles [83] which hydrolyzed NaBH4 with the maximal normalized rate of 96800 mL min−1·gcat −1 at 45 °C, and 52910 mL min−1·gcat −1 at 25 °C. However, the volume of the produced H2 at 25 °C in the presence of this catalyst was 68 % of the theoretically expected volume of hydrogen while CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nano-catalyst gathered 86 % of the expected volume of H2 at room temperature. Ni/rGO film [84] that has similar constituents to our synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nano-catalyst, reached the maximal normalized hydrolysis rate of 33000 mL min−1·gcat −1 at 21 °C and the efficiency of 69 %. In this case, the considerable merit of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni involves the higher yield of 86 % hydrogen. Additionally, it was discussed in Ref. [84] that Ni/rGO had expedited the reaction, and the hydrolysis of NaBH4 in presence of rGO had been trivial. Contrary to this report, as mentioned above, a macroscopic examination showed our synthesized CDs and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4 were also highly active catalysts of NaBH4 hydrolysis. Therefore, the merit of synergistic catalytic activities exists within the scaffold of the CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni catalyst. Another similar catalyst to compare is Co–Ni/rGO which majorly involves two catalytic components of cobalt and nickel. Here, the highest normalized hydrolysis rate for 0.5 g NaBH4 in the presence of 0.05 g catalyst was 1280 mL min−1·gcat −1 at 40 °C and the hydrogen yield was 70 %. These outcomes maybe are comparable to the hydrolysis of 0.3 g sodium borohydride in presence of 0.008 g CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni at 50 °C that contributed the maximal normalized rate of 13125 mL min−1·gcat −1 and 84 % hydrogen yield. Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 nano-cubes [79] accelerated the hydrolysis reaction up to 28000 mL min−1·gcat −1 at 30 °C; however, the reaction's yield is as low as 52 % and the active catalytic active site is the expensive Pd metal. In Ref. [52], B, N co-doped CDs delivered the hydrolysis reaction rate of 366 mL min−1·gcat −1 at 40 °C and a yield of 108 % within 100 min with the reaction condition including 5 mL H2O, 0.038 g NaBH4, and 0.007 g catalyst. In this study, the highest reached reaction rate is comparatively low. The study merits the over-expected volume of produced hydrogen but the time necessary for the reaction completion is long. These explained examples are the most outstanding catalysts that were given in Table 2 for comparison. Again, these results show the high activity of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nano-catalyst. The other catalysts given in Table 2 are in line with or much less active than the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni. For more comparisons one can refer to Refs. [4,39,85] which also approve this climax.

To validate hydrogen generation from NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction, a round bottomed flask containing 0.5 g NaBH4, 0.008 g CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni, and 10 mL H2O with a temperature set at 80 °C was installed. Then, the flask was equipped with a relatively narrow gas outlet. After 2 min of reaction continuation and washing inside space of the flask by the generated H2 stream, the gas outlet was blocked. Then, a 1 mL sample was taken from the flask and was injected into the GC apparatus. As brought in Fig. 10, H2 peak was registered with an area of 650.6018 which is a little smaller compared to the standard reference peak of 1 mL pure H2 with an area of 710.8550. The registered area for the experimental sample is equivalent to 0.9152 mL H2. The lower amount of hydrogen registered for the experimental sample can be attributed to presence of oxygen gas from water-splitting side reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrolysis mechanism of NabH4 in the presence of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni
ce:section.ce:para:
Two major models, Langmuir-Hinshelwood and Michaelis-Menten kinetic models, were reported in the literature for the hydrolysis of NaBH4. Based on the reports, metal-based catalysts generally follow the Langmuir–Hinshelwood model. Moreover, here and in our previous works, we established that the catalytic activities of our synthesized CDs/NDs composition (referred as GO and GOQDs in our previous works) was synergistically empowered in its composite forms. Therefore, we can propose the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model is the mechanism that was involved in the NaBH4 hydrolysis reactions carried out here in the presence of the synthesized CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nanocomposite (Scheme 1). On the basis of this model, we can propose that CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nano-catalyst has three different active sites; CDs/NDs system, Fe3O4, and Ni. Within this mechanism, we predict that NaBH4 and H2O are distinctly adsorbed to one of the three different active sites. Then, the hydride (
H −
) separated from
BH 4 −
reacts with the adjacent adsorbed water molecule to produce H2 and (
OH −
). Interaction between the produced (
OH −
) ion and the
BH 4 −
forms the
BH 3
( OH )
−
ion. Three similar successive steps via the same procedure are fulfilled which leads to replacement of all the hydrogen in the
BH 3
( OH )
−
ion by (
OH −
) ions to form
B
( OH )
4 −
, and four hydrogen molecules are generated, overall [101–103].

0.1 g NaBH4, 10 mL deionized water and 0.016 g CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni was applied to test the reusability feature of the nano-catalyst at 80 °C. After the completion of a reaction, the catalyst was isolated, washed, dried and reused at the next runs. As displayed in Fig. 11, the examination was repeated for 4 cycles, and no large decrease was observed in the highest H2 generation rate of the test reactions. Therefore, CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni expedited and speeded up all of the reusability test reactions without any large changes in its activity. An ICP-OES analysis revealed 3 % of Ni content was leached out from the initial composition of CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni after 4 cycles.

In this paper, CDs/NDs and CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nanocomposite were synthesized and characterized by FT-IR, SEM, EDX, mapping, TEM, XRD, TGA, VSM, BET and ICP-OES analyses. Performance of the prepared CDs/NDs and magnetic nano-catalyst was examined in NaBH4 hydrolysis. Examining macroscopically, our synthesized CDs/NDs was found to be a highly active but unstable catalyst of NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction. The results also showed that CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni efficiently expedites and speeds up NaBH4 hydrolysis and releases hydrogen expertly. The CDs/NDs@Fe3O4@Ni nanocomposite showed a perfect catalytic activity in proximity with the highest values reported so far with regard to its achieved hydrogen generation rate of 80000 mL min−1·gcat −1 and especially with regard to its high efficiencies. Moreover, the applied nano-catalyst was easily recovered using a magnet and reused for 4 cycles without any sensible loss of its catalytic activity in H2 generation from sodium borohydride hydrolysis. In consistent with our previous patented work [55], the effect of CDs/NDs foundation was apparent at higher temperatures where the produced H2 volume was drastically more than the theoretically expected values because of simultaneous H2 evolutions through CDs/NDs directed water-splitting processes.

In the context of future work, a wide variety of nano-catalysts based on CDs/NDs can be fabricated with a simultaneous focus on the properties that contribute to CDs/NDs composites with more efficiency towards NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction.
